questions.question_text: When priorities are discussed, how visible is the customer point of view?

options.answer: It is part of most discussions
It is raised in some discussions
It comes up mainly when there is a problem
It is rarely part of the conversation

questions.question_text: Is there a clear plan to improve the customer experience?

options.answer: Yes, but only some teams know it
There are ideas, but no clear plan

questions.question_text: Who usually takes the lead on customer experience topics?

options.answer: It changes depending on the topic

questions.question_text: How do people learn what a good customer experience looks like here?

options.answer: Through clear guidance and regular support
Through some guidance, depending on the team
Mostly by learning from others around them
There is no real guidance today

questions.question_text: How often do you collect customer feedback?

options.answer: Only when an issue comes up

questions.question_text: Once feedback is collected, what usually happens next?

options.answer: It is reviewed, analyzed, and turned into actions
It is looked at case by case
It is stored, but not really used

questions.question_text: Which journeys or moments do you track today?

options.answer: We do not really track journeys today

questions.question_text: How often is your view of the customer journey updated?

options.answer: It is not updated in a structured way

questions.question_text: How do you decide which journeys or moments matter most?

options.answer: Using clear criteria based on impact and evidence
Based on a mix of data and judgment
There is no clear way today

questions.question_text: When a pain point shows up, what usually happens?
After a change is made, how do you know if it really helped?

options.answer: We usually review the biggest changes
We get a general sense of whether it worked
We rarely go back and check

questions.question_text: When improvements are made, how is their business value measured?

Manage.description: No clear direction yet; ownership and guidance are limited or uneven.
Manage.recommendations:
This usually means the basics are not yet fully in place. Direction may be unclear, ownership may be fragmented, and teams may not have enough guidance.

Priority actions:
- Define one clear owner or a small leading group
- Set a simple shared direction for the customer experience
- Make leadership involvement more visible
- Give teams clearer guidance on what good looks like
- Bring the customer perspective more often into team decisions

Manage.description: Some structure exists, but not consistently across teams.
Manage.recommendations:
This usually means some foundations exist, but not in a consistent way across the organization.

Priority actions:
- Align teams around one shared direction
- Clarify responsibilities and decision roles
- Strengthen leadership follow-up on key topics
- Standardize guidance and support across teams
- Create more regular ways for teams to work together

Manage.description: Clear direction and ownership are in place.
Manage.recommendations:
This usually means direction, ownership, and support are already present. The focus now is on making them stronger and more sustainable.

Priority actions:
- Strengthen governance and accountability
- Embed customer thinking into daily decisions
- Keep leadership engaged over time
- Scale good practices across teams
- Connect direction more closely to delivery

Analyze.description: Limited visibility on experience, feedback, and journeys.
Analyze.recommendations:
This usually means the organization has only a limited view of the experience. Feedback may be collected occasionally, journeys may be unclear, and decisions may rely more on opinion than evidence.

Priority actions:
- Set up regular ways to gather feedback
- Identify a few key journey moments to track
- Create a shared view of what customers go through
- Introduce a basic set of measures across main channels
- Build a simple routine to review and use insights

Analyze.description: Some insight exists, but it is still partial or fragmented.
Analyze.recommendations:
This usually means some feedback, measurement, and journey tracking are in place, but the view is still partial or fragmented.

Priority actions:
- Bring feedback sources together more consistently
- Improve the way insights are reviewed and shared
- Refresh journey views more often
- Use clearer criteria to decide which moments matter most
- Help teams work from one shared picture

Analyze.description: Strong understanding of the experience across feedback, journeys, and measures.
Analyze.recommendations:
This usually means the organization has a strong view of feedback, journeys, and measures. The next step is to make insight sharper and more actionable.

Priority actions:
- Strengthen root-cause analysis
- Connect journey, channel, and operational views
- Make measures more useful for action, not only reporting
- Improve cross-team visibility
- Use insight to predict and prevent issues earlier

Improve.description: Issues are identified, but they do not consistently turn into action.
Improve.recommendations:
This usually means pain points are visible, but they do not consistently turn into action. Follow-up may be weak, results may not be checked, and business value may not be measured.

Priority actions:
- Create a simple action path for known issues
- Make ownership visible from the start
- Follow actions through to completion
- Check whether changes actually helped
- Start linking improvements to business value

Improve.description: Improvements happen, but not always in a repeatable way.
Improve.recommendations:
This usually means improvements happen, but not always in the same way. Some actions are tracked and some results are reviewed, but the approach is still uneven.

Priority actions:
- Make the path from issue to action more consistent
- Define a clearer way to review impact
- Measure business value for the main improvements
- Reduce dependence on individual effort
- Turn improvement into a regular operating habit

Improve.description: Improvements are acted on, reviewed, and measured in many cases.
Improve.recommendations:
This usually means the organization can move from pain points to action, review whether changes worked, and show value in many cases.

Priority actions:
- Scale successful improvements across teams and journeys
- Make learning from changes more systematic
- Link improvements more tightly to value creation
- Strengthen decision-making with evidence
- Move from isolated fixes to ongoing improvement cycles

answer_recommendations.question_text: When priorities are discussed, how visible is the customer point of view?
answer_recommendations.selected_answer: It is part of most discussions
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: The customer perspective is already present in decision-making.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Keep this strength visible. Build on it by making customer impact part of decision templates, project reviews, and leadership discussions so it stays consistent even when priorities shift.

answer_recommendations.question_text: When priorities are discussed, how visible is the customer point of view?
answer_recommendations.selected_answer: It is raised in some discussions
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: The customer perspective is present, but not yet consistent.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Create a simple habit that makes the customer perspective harder to miss. For example, add one customer-focused question to steering meetings, business reviews, and project sign-off moments.

answer_recommendations.question_text: When priorities are discussed, how visible is the customer point of view?
answer_recommendations.selected_answer: It comes up mainly when there is a problem
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: The organization is still reacting more than planning ahead.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Shift from reactive to proactive discussions. Use regular feedback reviews, journey reviews, or monthly pain-point discussions so customer topics come up before issues escalate.

answer_recommendations.question_text: When priorities are discussed, how visible is the customer point of view?
answer_recommendations.selected_answer: It is rarely part of the conversation
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: Customer topics are not yet part of the normal decision rhythm.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Start small but make it visible. Introduce a basic customer lens in leadership and team meetings, such as one recent pain point, one customer trend, or one journey issue to review regularly.

answer_recommendations.question_text: Is there a clear plan to improve the customer experience?
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: There is a shared direction in place.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Keep the plan alive by linking it to owners, milestones, and a small set of measures. Review progress regularly so it stays practical and does not become a static document.

answer_recommendations.question_text: Is there a clear plan to improve the customer experience?
answer_recommendations.selected_answer: Yes, but only some teams know it
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: The direction exists, but it is not yet fully shared.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Translate the plan into simple messages for each team: what matters, why it matters, and what each team can influence. This usually improves alignment quickly.

answer_recommendations.question_text: Is there a clear plan to improve the customer experience?
answer_recommendations.selected_answer: There are ideas, but no clear plan
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: Intent exists, but it has not been turned into a shared roadmap.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Turn scattered ideas into a short improvement plan with three parts: priority focus areas, owners, and the first actions to launch. Keep it simple and visible.

answer_recommendations.question_text: Is there a clear plan to improve the customer experience?
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: There is no shared direction yet.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Start with a lightweight plan rather than a full program. Define the main pain points to focus on, one sponsor, and a short list of priority actions for the next few months.

answer_recommendations.question_text: Who usually takes the lead on customer experience topics?
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: Ownership is established.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Make sure this role has enough visibility and support to coordinate across teams. Clear ownership works best when paired with leadership backing and a regular decision forum.

answer_recommendations.question_text: Who usually takes the lead on customer experience topics?
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: Responsibility exists, but shared ownership may create gaps.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Clarify who leads, who contributes, and who decides. A simple RACI or role note can reduce confusion and speed up action.

answer_recommendations.question_text: Who usually takes the lead on customer experience topics?
answer_recommendations.selected_answer: It changes depending on the topic
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: Ownership is flexible, but may be inconsistent.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Define a stable coordination point even if business ownership changes by topic. This helps maintain continuity across issues, journeys, and improvement work.

answer_recommendations.question_text: Who usually takes the lead on customer experience topics?
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: A lack of ownership will slow down action and follow-through.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Name a clear sponsor or coordinating team. Even a small, part-time ownership model is better than leaving the topic fully informal.

answer_recommendations.question_text: How do people learn what a good customer experience looks like here?
answer_recommendations.selected_answer: Through clear guidance and regular support
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: Teams are being enabled in a structured way.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Keep this practical by refreshing guidance with real examples, success stories, and role-based coaching so it stays relevant to daily work.

answer_recommendations.question_text: How do people learn what a good customer experience looks like here?
answer_recommendations.selected_answer: Through some guidance, depending on the team
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: Support exists, but not equally across the organization.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Identify where guidance is strongest and reuse those materials more widely. A small core toolkit can help reduce uneven maturity between teams.

answer_recommendations.question_text: How do people learn what a good customer experience looks like here?
answer_recommendations.selected_answer: Mostly by learning from others around them
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: Learning happens informally, which may create uneven standards.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Turn informal know-how into simple shared guidance. This can be as basic as a short playbook, examples of good practice, or onboarding support for key teams.

answer_recommendations.question_text: How do people learn what a good customer experience looks like here?
answer_recommendations.selected_answer: There is no real guidance today
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: Expectations are likely unclear across teams.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Start by defining what good looks like in a few priority moments or roles. Keep it simple, visible, and tied to real customer situations so teams can act on it.

answer_recommendations.question_text: How often do you collect customer feedback?
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: Feedback is captured consistently and close to real moments.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Keep the rhythm, but make sure it stays purposeful. Review whether you are capturing the right moments, avoiding survey fatigue, and linking feedback back to the journeys that matter most.

answer_recommendations.question_text: How often do you collect customer feedback?
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: There is a listening rhythm in place, but it may miss some key moments.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Look at where feedback is collected today and where important moments are still invisible. Often the next step is not collecting more everywhere, but collecting at the moments that have the biggest impact on the experience.

answer_recommendations.question_text: How often do you collect customer feedback?
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: Customer input is available, but not often enough to build a stable view.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Create a lighter but more regular listening rhythm. Start with a few recurring touchpoints or key interactions so teams can see patterns over time rather than isolated comments.

answer_recommendations.question_text: How often do you collect customer feedback?
answer_recommendations.selected_answer: Only when an issue comes up
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: The organization is mostly hearing from customers when something goes wrong.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Move from reactive listening to more balanced listening. Keep complaint-based input, but add a few regular feedback moments so teams can understand the experience before issues escalate.

answer_recommendations.question_text: How often do you collect customer feedback?
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: There is no structured listening approach in place today.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Start simple. Choose one or two critical moments in the customer journey and create a basic way to collect input there. The goal is not to launch a large program immediately, but to begin building a regular signal.

answer_recommendations.question_text: Once feedback is collected, what usually happens next?
answer_recommendations.selected_answer: It is reviewed, analyzed, and turned into actions
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: Feedback is already being used as a source of insight and action.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Keep strengthening the loop between listening and action. Make sure teams can see not just the comments themselves, but the patterns, root causes, and priorities that come out of them.

answer_recommendations.question_text: Once feedback is collected, what usually happens next?
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: Feedback is being looked at, but not always turned into clear action.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Push the process one step further. In addition to summary reports, define who reviews themes, who decides what matters, and what kind of actions should follow.

answer_recommendations.question_text: Once feedback is collected, what usually happens next?
answer_recommendations.selected_answer: It is looked at case by case
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: The organization is responding to individual signals, but not yet building a broader view.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Introduce a regular theme review. Even a monthly discussion of recurring issues, repeated comments, or low-performing moments can shift the organization from case-by-case reaction to insight-led improvement.

answer_recommendations.question_text: Once feedback is collected, what usually happens next?
answer_recommendations.selected_answer: It is stored, but not really used
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: Feedback is available, but it is not yet helping teams understand the experience.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Focus on use, not volume. Create a simple review rhythm with owners and one clear output: what did we learn, what matters most, and what should happen next.

answer_recommendations.question_text: Which journeys or moments do you track today?
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: The organization is looking at the experience across multiple steps and channels.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Build on this by keeping those journeys practical: link them to measures, feedback, and known pain points so they remain useful in decision-making, not just documentation.

answer_recommendations.question_text: Which journeys or moments do you track today?
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: There is focus on the main journeys, which is often a strong starting point.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Make sure the chosen journeys truly reflect where value and friction are highest. Over time, add missing journeys only when the current ones are actively used.

answer_recommendations.question_text: Which journeys or moments do you track today?
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: The view is still fragmented around separate moments rather than the full journey.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Start connecting touchpoints into a simple end-to-end journey view. Even a basic map of the steps before and after a key interaction can reveal where issues really begin.

answer_recommendations.question_text: Which journeys or moments do you track today?
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: Journey understanding is currently shaped mainly by issues and complaints.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Balance the view by adding moments outside of service recovery. This helps teams understand not only where things fail, but also where expectations are formed and trust is built.

answer_recommendations.question_text: Which journeys or moments do you track today?
answer_recommendations.selected_answer: We do not really track journeys today
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: There is little shared view of what customers go through end to end.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Pick one priority journey and map it simply. Focus on the main steps, handoffs, and pain points. A single usable journey view is often more valuable than broad but unused mapping work.

answer_recommendations.question_text: How often is your view of the customer journey updated?
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: The journey view stays alive and can keep pace with change.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Keep this habit linked to evidence. The best updates are not only calendar-based, but also triggered by new feedback, channel changes, or shifts in customer behavior.

answer_recommendations.question_text: How often is your view of the customer journey updated?
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: Journey knowledge is refreshed, but not in a regular enough way to support all decisions.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Set a lighter review rhythm that teams can sustain. Even a quarterly review of one or two key journeys can make the view more useful over time.

answer_recommendations.question_text: How often is your view of the customer journey updated?
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: The journey view risks becoming outdated and less useful for action.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Choose a regular point to revisit the journey, especially after major process, channel, or policy changes. The goal is to keep the view usable, not perfect.

answer_recommendations.question_text: How often is your view of the customer journey updated?
answer_recommendations.selected_answer: It is not updated in a structured way
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: Journey understanding depends too much on informal knowledge and memory.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Introduce a simple review trigger. For example, revisit the journey after repeated complaints, a drop in performance, or a major operational change.

answer_recommendations.question_text: How do you decide which journeys or moments matter most?
answer_recommendations.selected_answer: Using clear criteria based on impact and evidence
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: Priorities are set using a mature, evidence-based approach.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Keep the criteria visible and consistent. Make sure teams understand how customer impact, business value, and evidence come together when priorities are chosen.

answer_recommendations.question_text: How do you decide which journeys or moments matter most?
answer_recommendations.selected_answer: Based on a mix of data and judgment
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: There is some structure in prioritization, but it may still vary by team or context.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Tighten the approach by agreeing on a few shared criteria. Even a simple decision lens can improve consistency across teams and reduce debate based only on opinion.

answer_recommendations.question_text: How do you decide which journeys or moments matter most?
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: Priorities are driven more by internal perspective than by evidence from the experience itself.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Bring customer and operational evidence into prioritization discussions. A few shared measures, comments, or journey pain points can quickly improve the quality of decisions.

answer_recommendations.question_text: How do you decide which journeys or moments matter most?
answer_recommendations.selected_answer: There is no clear way today
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: There is no shared prioritization logic in place.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Start with a simple framework: what is affecting customers most, what is affecting the business most, and where action is realistic. This creates a clearer basis for deciding where to focus first.

answer_recommendations.question_text: When a pain point shows up, what usually happens?
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: Issues are moving into action with relatively little friction.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Protect this speed by making the action path repeatable. Define how issues are logged, who picks them up, and how teams know when a pain point has moved into delivery.

answer_recommendations.question_text: When a pain point shows up, what usually happens?
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: There is a response pattern, but not always the same one.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Reduce variation in the response path. A simple triage process with owners, timeframes, and escalation rules will make improvement more reliable.

answer_recommendations.question_text: When a pain point shows up, what usually happens?
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: Pain points are visible, but action may slow down at the discussion stage.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Create a clearer bridge from discussion to action. For example, agree on what qualifies for action, who decides, and what the first next step should be after a pain point is raised.

answer_recommendations.question_text: When a pain point shows up, what usually happens?
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: Known issues are not consistently turning into action.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Start with a simple action loop: capture the pain point, assign an owner, define the next step, and review open items regularly. This is often the fastest way to unblock progress.

answer_recommendations.question_text: After a change is made, how do you know if it really helped?
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: Impact checking is already part of the way of working.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Keep this strength and make it visible. Use before-and-after measures, feedback trends, or journey outcomes so teams can learn faster from what works.

answer_recommendations.question_text: After a change is made, how do you know if it really helped?
answer_recommendations.selected_answer: We usually review the biggest changes
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: Some impact review is in place, but mainly for larger initiatives.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Expand this habit to more day-to-day improvements. A lightweight review for smaller changes can improve learning without adding much overhead.

answer_recommendations.question_text: After a change is made, how do you know if it really helped?
answer_recommendations.selected_answer: We get a general sense of whether it worked
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: Learning is happening informally rather than through evidence.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Move from intuition to a simple evidence base. Choose one or two measures per improvement so teams can tell whether the change truly helped.

answer_recommendations.question_text: After a change is made, how do you know if it really helped?
answer_recommendations.selected_answer: We rarely go back and check
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: The loop is not really closed after implementation.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Introduce a light post-change review. Even one follow-up check after launch can improve learning, help teams adjust faster, and avoid repeating the same issues.

answer_recommendations.question_text: When improvements are made, how is their business value measured?
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: The organization is able to connect improvement work to business impact.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Keep the approach practical and repeatable. Use it to prioritize future investments and communicate value to sponsors and leaders.

answer_recommendations.question_text: When improvements are made, how is their business value measured?
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: Value is measured, but not yet systematically.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Identify which types of improvement should always include a value view, such as high-volume issues, major journeys, or investment-heavy changes. This helps make value measurement more consistent.

answer_recommendations.question_text: When improvements are made, how is their business value measured?
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: There is some sense of value, but it is not yet structured.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Start with simple value logic rather than a complex business case. Link improvements to a small set of outcomes such as fewer complaints, lower cost-to-serve, better conversion, or better retention.

answer_recommendations.question_text: When improvements are made, how is their business value measured?
answer_recommendations.what_this_suggests: Improvement work is not yet connected to business value.
answer_recommendations.detailed_recommendation: Begin with a few visible examples. Choose one or two recent improvements and estimate their effect on service cost, revenue, retention, or operational effort to build the habit of value measurement.

flow_transitions.selected_answer: A1 - It is part of most discussions

flow_transitions.selected_option_label: It is part of most discussions

flow_transitions.selected_answer: B3 - There are ideas, but no clear plan
flow_transitions.transition: That makes sense. If there are ideas on the table but no shared plan yet, who usually pulls these topics forward?
flow_transitions.selected_option_label: There are ideas, but no clear plan

flow_transitions.selected_answer: B4 - No clear plan
flow_transitions.transition: Thanks, that’s helpful to know. Without a clear plan today, who usually takes the lead on customer experience topics?

flow_transitions.selected_answer: C1 - One clear person or team

flow_transitions.selected_answer: C2 - A few teams share it
flow_transitions.transition: That helps. Since a few teams share the role, how do people usually learn what good customer experience looks like here?

flow_transitions.selected_answer: C3 - It changes depending on the topic
flow_transitions.transition: Understood. If ownership shifts depending on the topic, how do people usually learn what good customer experience looks like?
flow_transitions.selected_option_label: It changes depending on the topic

flow_transitions.selected_answer: C4 - It is not really clear
flow_transitions.transition: I see. If ownership is not very clear today, how do people learn what good customer experience looks like in practice?

flow_transitions.selected_answer: E1 - Continuously or after key interactions
flow_transitions.transition: That is a strong starting point. You are hearing from customers at the moments that matter most. Once that feedback comes in, what usually happens next?

flow_transitions.selected_answer: E2 - Regularly, on a planned basis
flow_transitions.transition: That helps. So there is already a regular rhythm in place. Once feedback comes in, what do you usually do with it?

flow_transitions.selected_answer: E3 - From time to time
flow_transitions.transition: Got it. So feedback is gathered at certain moments, but not all the time. When it does come in, what usually happens next?

flow_transitions.selected_answer: E4 - Only when an issue comes up
flow_transitions.transition: That makes sense. So feedback mostly appears when something needs attention. When that happens, what do you usually do next?
flow_transitions.selected_option_label: Only when an issue comes up

flow_transitions.selected_answer: E5 - We do not collect it
flow_transitions.transition: Thanks, that is helpful to know. If customer input comes in through complaints, teams, or day-to-day interactions, what usually happens with it?

flow_transitions.selected_answer: F1 - It is reviewed, analyzed, and turned into actions
flow_transitions.transition: That is a solid base. It means feedback is already feeding decisions. When you look across the experience, what journeys or moments do you actually track today?
flow_transitions.selected_option_label: It is reviewed, analyzed, and turned into actions

flow_transitions.selected_answer: F2 - It is reviewed and summarized
flow_transitions.transition: That is a useful start. So there is already some structure in how feedback is handled. What journeys or moments do you tend to track today?

flow_transitions.selected_answer: F3 - It is looked at case by case
flow_transitions.transition: I see. So the picture is built one issue at a time today. What journeys or moments do you usually keep an eye on?
flow_transitions.selected_option_label: It is looked at case by case

flow_transitions.selected_answer: F4 - It is stored, but not really used
flow_transitions.transition: Understood. So the challenge is not only hearing customers, but making use of what is heard. What journeys or moments do you track today, if any?
flow_transitions.selected_option_label: It is stored, but not really used

flow_transitions.selected_answer: G1 - End-to-end journeys across several touchpoints
flow_transitions.transition: That gives you quite a complete view. How often do you go back and refresh that journey view?

flow_transitions.selected_answer: G2 - A few important journeys
flow_transitions.transition: That makes sense. Focusing on the key journeys first is often a practical way to start. How often do you revisit that view?

flow_transitions.selected_answer: G3 - Individual touchpoints only
flow_transitions.transition: Got it. So the view is still centered on specific touchpoints more than the full journey. How often is that view updated?

flow_transitions.selected_answer: G4 - Mostly service or complaint moments
flow_transitions.transition: Understood. So you mostly see the experience through the moments where things go wrong. How often do you step back and update that view?

flow_transitions.selected_answer: G5 - We do not really track journeys today
flow_transitions.transition: Thanks, that gives a clear picture. If there is any journey view today, how often does it actually get reviewed or updated?
flow_transitions.selected_option_label: We do not really track journeys today

flow_transitions.selected_answer: H1 - Regularly
flow_transitions.transition: That is a positive sign. It suggests the journey view stays alive over time. When you decide where to focus, how do you decide which journeys or moments matter most?

flow_transitions.selected_answer: H2 - Sometimes
flow_transitions.transition: That helps. So the journey view gets refreshed from time to time. When you decide where to focus, how do you usually make that choice?

flow_transitions.selected_answer: H3 - Rarely
flow_transitions.transition: Got it. So updates happen, but not very often. In that context, how do you decide which journeys or moments deserve attention first?

flow_transitions.selected_answer: H4 - It is not updated in a structured way
flow_transitions.transition: I see. So keeping focus on the right moments becomes even more important. How do you usually decide what matters most?
flow_transitions.selected_option_label: It is not updated in a structured way

flow_transitions.selected_answer: E1 - It turns into action quickly
flow_transitions.transition: That’s a positive sign. When action happens quickly, how do you usually tell whether the change really made a difference?

flow_transitions.selected_answer: E2 - It usually gets picked up
flow_transitions.transition: That helps. So issues are usually picked up. Once something is changed, how do you normally tell whether it really helped?

flow_transitions.selected_answer: E3 - It may be discussed first
flow_transitions.transition: Understood. So there is often a discussion before things move. When a change does happen, how do you usually know whether it helped?

flow_transitions.selected_answer: E4 - It often stays unresolved
flow_transitions.transition: Thanks for being open about that. When improvements do happen, how do you usually tell whether they made things better?

flow_transitions.selected_answer: F1 - We check results clearly
flow_transitions.transition: That’s a strong habit. And when improvements are made, how is their business value usually measured?

flow_transitions.selected_answer: F2 - We usually review the biggest changes
flow_transitions.transition: That makes sense. So the bigger changes get reviewed. When improvements are made, how is their business value usually measured?
flow_transitions.selected_option_label: We usually review the biggest changes

flow_transitions.selected_answer: F3 - We get a general sense of whether it worked
flow_transitions.transition: Got it. So there is usually a feel for what worked, even if it is not always formal. When improvements are made, how is their business value measured?
flow_transitions.selected_option_label: We get a general sense of whether it worked

flow_transitions.selected_answer: F4 - We rarely go back and check
flow_transitions.transition: I see. So follow-up is still limited today. When improvements are made, how is their business value measured?
flow_transitions.selected_option_label: We rarely go back and check

benchmark_layer.purpose: Compare internal maturity results with real-world tools and competitors
